skill.name: async-sync-advisor
skill.description: Guides users on choosing between async and sync patterns for Lambda functions, including when to use tokio, rayon, and spawn_blocking. Activates when users write Lambda handlers with mixed workloads.
skill.summary: Advises Rust Lambda developers on async, blocking, and parallel workload patterns.
skill.author: EmilLindfors

security_audit.summary: Static analysis flagged Ruby-style backticks and weak cryptography indicators, but review found only Markdown code fences, Rust identifiers, and descriptive text. No executable scripts, prompt injection attempts, credential access, network exfiltration, or dangerous tool permissions were found in SKILL.md.

low_findings.title: External Command Static Findings Are Markdown False Positives
low_findings.description: Verdict: FALSE_POSITIVE. The flagged backticks are Markdown inline code and fenced Rust examples, not Ruby shell execution or executable skill code. The skill only declares Read and Grep tools.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The locations are visible Markdown or Rust snippets in SKILL.md. There is no shell command string, Ruby code, script file, or tool permission that would execute these snippets.

low_findings.title: Weak Cryptography Static Finding Is Descriptive Text
low_findings.description: Verdict: FALSE_POSITIVE. The high-severity weak cryptography alert points to the frontmatter description, which describes async and sync Lambda advice. No cryptographic algorithm implementation or recommendation appears at that location.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The flagged line is metadata text about async and sync patterns. I did not find evidence of MD5, SHA1, DES, RC4, or similar weak cryptographic usage in the reviewed file.

content.user_title: Choose Async or Sync for Rust Lambda
content.value_statement: Rust Lambda functions can waste time when CPU work blocks the async runtime. This skill helps Claude, Codex, and Claude Code choose async, spawn_blocking, or Rayon patterns for each workload.
actual_capabilities: Classifies Lambda handler work as I/O-bound, CPU-bound, or mixed.
Recommends async patterns for HTTP, database, S3, and DynamoDB calls.
Suggests tokio spawn_blocking for synchronous CPU-heavy work.
Shows when Rayon can parallelize large collection processing.
Explains performance tradeoffs for mixed I/O and compute phases.
limitations: Focuses on Rust Lambda handlers, not every Rust server application.
Provides guidance and examples, not automated benchmarking.
Does not inspect runtime metrics unless the user supplies them.
Does not rewrite full projects without additional user context.

use_cases.title: Review a New Handler
use_cases.description: Check whether a Rust Lambda handler should use async calls, blocking work, or a mixed pattern.

use_cases.title: Improve Cold Path Performance
use_cases.description: Identify CPU-heavy sections that should move away from the async runtime.

use_cases.title: Teach Concurrency Choices
use_cases.description: Explain why tokio, spawn_blocking, and Rayon fit different Lambda workloads.

prompt_templates.title: Classify My Handler
prompt_templates.scenario: Beginner review of one Lambda handler.
prompt_templates.prompt: Review this Rust Lambda handler and classify the workload as I/O-bound, CPU-bound, or mixed. Recommend async, spawn_blocking, or Rayon.

prompt_templates.title: Fix Blocking Work
prompt_templates.prompt: Find any CPU-heavy operations that may block the tokio runtime. Suggest where spawn_blocking should be used and explain the tradeoffs.

prompt_templates.title: Design Mixed Workload Flow
prompt_templates.scenario: Advanced handler design with multiple phases.
prompt_templates.prompt: Design a concurrency pattern for a Lambda handler that downloads data, processes it in parallel, and uploads results. Explain each phase.

prompt_templates.title: Evaluate Production Tradeoffs
prompt_templates.prompt: Evaluate this Rust Lambda concurrency strategy for latency, memory pressure, thread usage, and error handling. Recommend concrete improvements.

output_examples.input: A handler fetches user, profile, and settings from separate services.
output_examples.output: Use async concurrency. The calls are independent I/O operations, so joining them can reduce total latency without blocking compute threads.

output_examples.input: A handler decodes images and transforms thousands of records.
output_examples.output: Use spawn_blocking for the CPU-heavy section. Add Rayon inside that section if the record transformations are independent and large enough.

output_examples.input: A handler downloads files, parses them, and writes results to S3.
output_examples.output: Use a mixed pattern. Keep downloads and uploads async, then isolate parsing in a blocking or parallel compute phase.

best_practices: Separate I/O phases from CPU phases before choosing a concurrency model.
Use spawn_blocking when synchronous compute would block the async runtime.
Use Rayon only when work can be split into independent parallel units.
anti_patterns: Do not make CPU-heavy work async only to match the handler signature.
Do not run independent I/O calls sequentially when they can safely join.
Do not add Rayon for small workloads where scheduling overhead dominates.

faq.question: Does this skill execute Rust code?
faq.answer: No. It provides review guidance and example patterns for Rust Lambda handlers.

faq.question: When should I use async in Lambda?
faq.answer: Use async for network, database, storage, and other I/O operations that spend time waiting.

faq.question: When should I use spawn_blocking?
faq.answer: Use spawn_blocking when CPU-heavy synchronous work would block the tokio runtime.

faq.question: When does Rayon help?
faq.answer: Rayon helps when large CPU-bound collections can be processed independently in parallel.

faq.question: Can this replace benchmarking?
faq.answer: No. It gives design guidance, but production tuning still needs measurement.

faq.question: Which tools can use this skill?
faq.answer: The report lists support for Claude, Codex, and Claude Code.
